methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $41M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Spiritus, a Series A climate and energy company founded by Charles Cadieu, has raised $41M to date. The valuation assessment triangulates across a peer universe of 9 private firms and 1 public comparable (LNZA), focusing on three same-sector, same-stage private companies: Graphyte ($200M), Fourth Power ($150M), and Steady Energy ($110M).

Two methods were applied. The stage-progression approach uses the $41M raised as a proxy base and applies a 2.0x multiplier typical of Series A to subsequent-stage step-ups, implying a $82M valuation. The peer-median method references the three same-sector, same-stage comparables, which span a $110M to $200M range with a median of $150M. This method implies $150M, reflecting market-clearing valuations for peers at identical stage and vertical.

The triangulated analysis supports a range of $70M to $172M, with a median of $116M. The stage-progression method anchors the low end; the peer-median anchors the high end. Key uncertainties include the representativeness of the three-company peer set, timing and premium/discount attributable to capital efficiency versus peer benchmarks, and volatility in climate-tech valuations. The median of $116M sits approximately one-third above the raised amount, consistent with typical Series A mark-ups, and falls within the interquartile range of observed peers.
